questions.question: What is the main motivation for code switching?

options.text: To reduce the number of qubits
To access transversal gates not available in a single code

questions.explanation: Code switching allows accessing different transversal gate sets from different codes. Since no single code has a transversal universal gate set (Eastin-Knill), switching between codes like Steane (Clifford gates) and Reed-Muller (T gate) enables universality.

questions.question: What is special about the Steane and Reed-Muller code pair for code switching?

options.text: They have the same number of qubits
The Steane code can be embedded in the Reed-Muller code

questions.explanation: The Steane [[7,1,3]] code can be embedded in the Reed-Muller [[15,1,3]] code because the underlying classical codes satisfy [7,4,3] ⊂ [15,11,3]. This makes switching between them relatively straightforward using Clifford operations.

questions.question: What is gauge fixing in subsystem codes?

options.text: Measuring gauge operators to change the effective code

questions.explanation: Gauge fixing means measuring the gauge operators (which are normally unfixed in subsystem codes) to lock them to specific values. This changes the effective code, potentially enabling different transversal gates.

questions.question: What is code deformation?

options.text: Gradually changing the code structure while maintaining fault tolerance

questions.explanation: Code deformation gradually morphs the code structure through small, fault-tolerant steps. This can include changing boundaries, moving defects, or smoothly transitioning between different code configurations.

questions.question: In defect braiding, what creates logical operations?

options.text: Moving defects around each other in specific patterns

questions.explanation: In topological codes, moving defects around each other (braiding) creates logical operations on the encoded information. Different braiding patterns correspond to different logical gates.

questions.question: When is code switching preferred over magic state injection?

options.text: When many T gates are needed
When few T gates are needed and clean transversal T is desired
When the threshold needs to be maximized

questions.explanation: Code switching is preferred when few T gates are needed (avoiding the factory overhead of magic states) and when the cleanest possible T gate is desired (transversal T has no distillation noise).

questions.question: Code switching requires completely re-encoding the logical qubit from scratch in the new code.
questions.explanation: False. Code switching uses the structural relationship between compatible codes (like embedding) to transfer information without full re-encoding. The process uses Clifford operations to map between code structures while preserving the logical information.
